1.notes:
Amos - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God introduces Amos the shepherd-prophet and pronounces judgment on six surrounding nations (Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon) for their crimes against humanity, using the escalating formula "for three transgressions and for four."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — God Judges the Nations
Key Themes: Universal divine justice, crimes against humanity,
the "for three... and for four" formula, fire as judgment, God
holds ALL nations accountable regardless of covenant relationship

Historical Context: Amos was a shepherd and fig-tree cultivator
from Tekoa (south of Bethlehem in Judah) called to prophesy to
the northern kingdom during the reign of Jeroboam II — a time of
unprecedented prosperity AND unprecedented social injustice. The
earthquake referenced (v.1) was so significant it was remembered
centuries later (Zechariah 14:5). Chapter 1 begins God's judgment
oracles against SIX surrounding nations: Damascus (Syria), Gaza
(Philistia), Tyre (Phoenicia), Edom, Ammon — building a spiral
that will ultimately land on ISRAEL itself (2:6). The audience
would have cheered each judgment on their enemies, not realizing
the trap being set.

Structure:
- Introduction: Amos the Shepherd-Prophet (v.1)
- The LORD Roars from Zion (v.2)
- Judgment on Damascus/Syria (vv.3-5)
- Judgment on Gaza/Philistia (vv.6-8)
- Judgment on Tyre/Phoenicia (vv.9-10)
- Judgment on Edom (vv.11-12)
- Judgment on Ammon (vv.13-15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 2 Kings     | 14      | 23-29              | The reign of Jeroboam II; material prosperity without spiritual health
v.2              | Joel        | 3       | 16                 | "The LORD roars from Zion" (ESV)
v.2              | Jeremiah    | 25      | 30                 | "The LORD will roar from on high" (ESV)
v.3              | 2 Kings     | 8       | 12                 | Hazael's cruelty to Israel: "you will dash in pieces their little ones and rip open their pregnant women" (ESV)
v.9              | 1 Kings     | 5       | 1,                 | 9:13 — The former brotherhood covenant between Israel and Tyre (Solomon and Hiram)
v.11             | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Obadiah 10-14 — Edom's violence against brother Jacob when Jerusalem fell.
v.11             | Genesis     | 27      | 41                 | "Esau hated Jacob" (ESV) — the original grudge

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "herdmen" (KJV) / "shepherds" (ESV) — Hebrew "noqed" = a specific type of sheep-breeder (used only here and of King Mesha of Moab). Amos was not poor but a prosperous livestock owner called from secular work to prophetic ministry.
v.1              | KJV         | "the earthquake" — So well-known it needed no further identification. Archaeological evidence confirms a major earthquake in Israel approximately 760 BC.
v.3              | KJV         | "for three... and for four" — A Hebrew numerical idiom (X / X+1) meaning "multiple" or "overflowing." The fourth is the climactic, tipping-point transgression.
v.3              | KJV         | "threshed Gilead with threshing instruments of iron" — Literal: dragged iron-studded threshing sledges over human bodies. Syria's cruelty to the Transjordan Israelites.
v.9              | KJV         | "the brotherly covenant" — Likely the alliance between Solomon and Hiram of Tyre (1 Kings 5:12). Tyre broke this ancient treaty of friendship.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God judges ALL nations, not just Israel (vv.3-15). No nation is exempt
   from divine accountability. Whether they know God's law or not, basic
   human decency is expected. Cruelty, slave- trading, treaty-breaking, and
   violence against the vulnerable will be judged regardless of the
   perpetrator's religion.

2. "For three... and for four" — God counts (v.3). He is patient, but He is
   not oblivious. Every transgression is recorded. There IS a tipping point.
   Don't mistake His patience for permission or His silence for approval.

3. The spiral of judgment moves inward. Amos starts with DISTANT nations
   (Damascus, Gaza) and spirals closer: Tyre (ally), Edom (cousin), Ammon
   (neighbor)... then Judah, then Israel. God's people are not exempt from
   His standards — they are MOST accountable. Judgment begins at the house
   of God (1 Peter 4:17).

4. Permanent anger is judged (v.11). Edom "kept his wrath forever."
   Unforgiveness held perpetually is not neutral — it is sin that God
   judges. Release it before it condemns you.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Amos Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/amos/

David Guzik — Amos 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/amos-1/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Spurgeon (Public Domain)

2.notes:
Amos - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The judgment spiral closes on Israel itself — despite God's deliverance from Egypt and provision through the wilderness, they silenced His prophets and corrupted His Nazirites, so now judgment is inescapable.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Trap Springs: Israel
Indicted
Key Themes: Judgment on Moab and Judah, the climactic indictment
of Israel, selling the poor, oppressing the afflicted, silencing
prophets, ingratitude for divine deliverance, inescapable judgment

Historical Context: Chapter 2 completes the spiral begun in
chapter 1. After Moab (v.1) and Judah (v.4), the hammer falls on
ISRAEL (v.6) — the ultimate target. The audience that cheered
judgment on their enemies is now indicted themselves. Israel's
sins are specifically SOCIAL: selling the righteous for silver,
trampling the poor, sexual immorality, exploitation through pledge-
taking. God then recounts His saving acts (Exodus, wilderness,
Amorite conquest, raising prophets/Nazirites) — making Israel's
ingratitude inexcusable. The chapter ends with terrifying
inevitability: no one escapes. Not the swift, not the strong, not
the mighty, not the horseman.

Structure:
- Judgment on Moab (vv.1-3)
- Judgment on Judah (vv.4-5)
- The CLIMAX: Judgment on Israel (vv.6-8)
- God's Past Faithfulness: Inexcusable Ingratitude (vv.9-12)
- Inescapable Judgment (vv.13-16)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | 2 Kings     | 17      | 13-15              | Judah rejecting God's law and following their fathers into idolatry
v.6              | Amos        | 8       | 6                  | "that we may buy the poor for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals" (ESV). Same charge repeated
v.7              | Isaiah      | 10      | 2                  | "to turn aside the needy from justice and to rob the poor of my people of their right" (ESV)
v.9              | Numbers     | 21      | 21-35              | God's defeat of the Amorites for Israel
v.10             | Deuteronomy | 8       | 2                  | "the LORD your God has led you these forty years in the wilderness" (ESV)
v.11             | Numbers     | 6       | 1-21               | The Nazirite vow: consecration to God
v.12             | Jeremiah    | 11      | 21                 | "Do not prophesy in the name of the LORD" (ESV). Silencing prophets
v.14             | Psalm       | 33      | 16-17              | "The king is not saved by his great army; a warrior is not delivered by his great strength" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.6              | KJV         | "sold the righteous for silver" — Hebrew "tsaddiq" = the one who is in the right (legally innocent). Judges take bribes to rule against the innocent. "A pair of shoes" = the price is trivial — human dignity sold for almost nothing.
v.7              | KJV         | "pant after the dust" (KJV) vs. "trample the head of the poor" (ESV) — The Hebrew is debated. Either: they DESIRE even the dust on the poor's head (their grief) or they TRAMPLE the poor's head into dust. Both indicate extreme cruelty toward the vulnerable.
v.8              | KJV         | "clothes laid to pledge" — Garments taken as loan collateral must be returned by nightfall (Exodus 22:26-27). Instead, they keep them and use them as bedding at pagan shrines. "Wine of the condemned" = wine purchased with fines extorted from the poor.
v.13             | KJV         | "pressed under you" (KJV) / "press you down" (ESV) — Hebrew "uq" is debated: either God is BURDENED by Israel (like an overloaded cart) or He will CRUSH Israel (like a cart crushing what's beneath it). ESV takes the second reading.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Greater privilege = greater accountability (vv.9-12). God reviews
   everything He did FOR Israel before listing what Israel did AGAINST Him.
   The more you've received, the more is expected. Your spiritual advantages
   increase your responsibility, not decrease your accountability.

2. "A pair of shoes" (v.6). How cheaply do you value people? The
   exploitation of the vulnerable for trivial gain is what MOST provokes
   God's judgment — not theological error, but INJUSTICE. How do you treat
   the powerless? That answer defines your standing before God.

3. They silenced the prophets (v.12). When you reject correction, avoid
   conviction, skip over uncomfortable scriptures, or dismiss the person who
   speaks hard truth — you are commanding the prophet: "Don't prophesy." Are
   you silencing God's voice in your life?

4. "The mighty shall flee away naked" (v.16). No human resource saves from
   divine judgment. Not wealth, not speed, not strength, not weapons. On
   that day, the most courageous warrior runs naked. Only God is refuge.
   Stop trusting in human capacities that will fail when it matters most.

3.notes:
Amos - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God declares that Israel's election brings greater accountability, not immunity — "You only have I known, therefore I will punish you" — and the prophet speaks because the Lord GOD has spoken; who can but prophesy?

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Privilege Means Accountability
Key Themes: Election means responsibility not immunity, cause and
effect in judgment, God reveals His plans to prophets, the wealthy
who store up violence, luxury destroyed

Historical Context: Chapter 3 establishes the theological basis
for God's judgment on Israel: because they are chosen ("You only
have I known"), they are MORE accountable, not less. Amos uses a
series of rhetorical cause-and-effect questions (vv.3-6) to prove
that his prophecy is not arbitrary — it has a CAUSE (Israel's sin)
and an inevitable EFFECT (judgment). He invites even pagan nations
(Ashdod, Egypt) to WITNESS Israel's corruption — it's so bad that
pagans would be shocked.

Structure:
- Election = Greater Accountability (vv.1-2)
- Cause and Effect: Seven Rhetorical Questions (vv.3-6)
- God Reveals His Plans to Prophets (vv.7-8)
- Pagan Witnesses to Israel's Corruption (vv.9-10)
- Judgment: Only Scraps Will Remain (vv.11-15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Luke        | 12      | 48                 | "Everyone to whom much was given, of him much will be required" (ESV)
v.2              | 1 Peter     | 4       | 17                 | "It is time for judgment to begin at the household of God" (ESV)
v.3              | 2 Corinthians | 6       | 14                 | "What fellowship has righteousness with lawlessness?" (ESV)
v.7              | Genesis     | 18      | 17                 | "Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?" (ESV). God reveals plans to His friends
v.7              | John        | 15      | 15                 | "All that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you" (ESV)
v.12             | Isaiah      | 1       | 9                  | "If the LORD of hosts had not left us a few survivors, we should have been like Sodom" (ESV)
v.15             | 1 Kings     | 22      | 39                 | Ahab's "ivory house" — the luxury that accompanied Israel's corruption

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "known" — Hebrew "yada" = intimate, relational knowing (not just cognitive awareness). God CHOSE Israel in intimate covenant. This makes their betrayal worse, not less serious.
v.12             | KJV         | "two legs, or a piece of an ear" — A shepherd recovers scraps from a lion to prove the animal was lost to predation. Only FRAGMENTS of Israel will survive. Not a rescue — just evidence of what was lost.
v.15             | KJV         | "houses of ivory" — Excavations at Samaria have uncovered ivory inlays in palatial homes, confirming this luxury. Built on oppression, destroyed in judgment.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "You only have I known — THEREFORE I will punish" (v.2). Your spiritual
   privileges (upbringing, Bible access, church, teaching) do not protect
   you — they EXPOSE you. Greater knowledge = greater responsibility. Stop
   assuming your background saves you.

2. "Can two walk together except they be agreed?" (v.3). You cannot walk
   with God while walking in the opposite direction. Agreement precedes
   fellowship. Where have you departed from God's ways? That's where the
   fellowship broke down.

3. God always warns before He acts (v.7). The fact that you're reading this,
   feeling convicted — THAT is God's warning. Don't ignore it. The prophet
   speaks before judgment falls. Respond now.

4.notes:
Amos - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God condemns the wealthy women of Samaria ("cows of Bashan") who oppress the poor, and recounts five warning judgments He sent — famine, drought, blight, plague, overthrow — yet "you did not return to Me."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — "Yet Have Ye Not Returned"
Key Themes: The "cows of Bashan" — wealthy women who oppress the
poor, sarcastic invitation to sin at Bethel, five failed
disciplines, "prepare to meet thy God"

Historical Context: Chapter 4 opens with one of the most
confrontational addresses in Scripture: the wealthy women of
Samaria are called "cows of Bashan" — pampered, fat cattle who
demand luxury while their husbands crush the poor. Then God
employs devastating SARCASM (vv.4-5): "Come to Bethel and SIN!"
He mocks their religious enthusiasm. The chapter's center is a
five-fold refrain: "yet have ye not returned unto me" (vv.6-11).
God sent famine, drought, blight, pestilence, and overthrow — five
escalating disciplines — and Israel ignored every one. The chapter
climaxes with the terrifying command: "Prepare to meet thy God,
O Israel" (v.12).

Structure:
- The Cows of Bashan: Luxury Through Oppression (vv.1-3)
- Sarcastic Invitation: Come to Bethel and Sin! (vv.4-5)
- Five Disciplines, Five Refusals to Return (vv.6-11)
- The Final Warning: Prepare to Meet Your God (vv.12-13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 22      | 12                 | "Strong bulls of Bashan surround me" (ESV)
v.6              | Haggai      | 1       | 6                  | "You have sown much, and harvested little" (ESV). Same pattern: God removes blessing to provoke return
v.11             | Genesis     | 19      | 24-25              | The overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah
v.11             | Zechariah   | 3       | 2                  | "Is not this a brand plucked from the fire?" (ESV). Same image of narrow escape
v.12             | Hebrews     | 10      | 31                 | "It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God" (ESV)
v.13             | Hebrews     | 4       | 13                 | "No creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "kine of Bashan" = "cows of Bashan." Bashan (modern Golan Heights) was famous for lush pastures and fat cattle (Deuteronomy 32:14, Psalm 22:12). The metaphor: pampered, overfed, demanding luxury.
v.4              | KJV         | "Come to Bethel, and transgress" — SARCASM. God mocks their religious enthusiasm. "You love your rituals? Fine — go ahead! Sin MORE at Bethel! Your 'worship' is transgression."
v.6-11           | KJV         | Five escalating disciplines: (1) famine (v.6), (2) drought (vv.7-8), (3) blight and locusts (v.9), (4) pestilence and war (v.10), (5) destruction like Sodom (v.11). Each followed by "yet have ye not returned."
v.13             | KJV         | "declareth unto man what is his thought" — God knows your THOUGHTS. Hebrew "sechoh" = meditation, inner thought. Nothing is hidden.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Yet have ye not returned unto me" — FIVE TIMES (vv.6-11). God sent
   increasing difficulty to bring you back. How many warnings have YOU
   received? Financial trouble, health scares, broken relationships — could
   these be God's "yet have ye not returned" in YOUR life? Stop ignoring the
   pattern.

2. "Prepare to meet thy God" (v.12). You WILL meet God. The only question is
   whether you meet Him as prepared or unprepared. Every day is preparation
   day. Are you ready?

3. Consumer luxury built on someone else's suffering (v.1). Whose pain funds
   your comfort? Exploitation doesn't always look dramatic — it looks like
   demanding more while caring less about where it comes from.

4. Religious enthusiasm can be pure transgression (vv.4-5). Going to church,
   giving offerings, being visibly spiritual — if your heart hasn't changed
   and the poor are still crushed, God sarcastically says: "Enjoy your sin!
   This is what you LOVE." Don't confuse religious activity with actual
   obedience.

5.notes:
Amos - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A funeral dirge over Israel calls them to seek the LORD and live, demanding justice over empty ritual — "Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — A Funeral Song and the Call to
Seek God
Key Themes: Lamentation over Israel's fall, "seek me and live,"
the God who makes the stars, injustice in the courts, let justice
roll down like waters, God hates empty worship, the Day of the
LORD as darkness

Historical Context: Chapter 5 is the HEART of Amos — containing
both its most desperate plea ("Seek ye me, and ye shall live,"
v.4) and its most famous verse ("Let judgment run down as waters,"
v.24). Amos sings a FUNERAL SONG (vv.1-3) over Israel — they are
already dead in God's eyes. Yet even now, the invitation stands:
SEEK and LIVE. The chapter condemns those who pervert justice in
the courts, trample the poor, and hate truth-tellers. It includes
a stunning doxology (v.8) praising the Creator. And it delivers
one of Scripture's most quoted prophetic demands: God wants
JUSTICE flowing like a river, not religious noise.

Structure:
- The Funeral Song: Israel Is Fallen (vv.1-3)
- The Invitation: Seek Me and Live (vv.4-6)
- Injustice Indicted: Wormwood for Justice (vv.7-13)
- The Call: Seek Good, Hate Evil, Love Justice (vv.14-15)
- Coming Wailing (vv.16-17)
- The Day of the LORD: Darkness, Not Light (vv.18-20)
- God Hates Empty Worship (vv.21-23)
- The River of Justice (v.24)
- Exile Announced (vv.25-27)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Deuteronomy | 4       | 29                 | "you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search after him with all your heart" (ESV)
v.8              | Job         | 9       | 9                  | "who made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades" (ESV)
v.14             | Micah       | 6       | 8                  | "do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God" (ESV)
v.18             | 2 Peter     | 3       | 10                 | "the day of the Lord will come like a thief" (ESV)
v.21             | Isaiah      | 1       | 11,14              | "I have had enough of burnt offerings of rams... Your new moons and your appointed feasts my soul hates" (ESV)
v.24             | Matthew     | 23      | 23                 | "you have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness" (ESV)
v.24             | James       | 1       | 27                 | "Religion that is pure and undefiled... to visit orphans and widows" (ESV)
v.26             | Acts        | 7       | 42-43              | Stephen quotes vv.25-27 in his speech before the Sanhedrin

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "turn judgment to wormwood" — Hebrew "la'anah" = wormwood, a bitter plant. Justice is made BITTER — corrupted from its intended sweetness into poison.
v.8              | KJV         | "seven stars and Orion" — Hebrew "kimah" (Pleiades) and "kesil" (Orion). The Creator of the cosmos is the one demanding justice. His authority is absolute.
v.15             | KJV         | "the remnant of Joseph" — Even at this late hour, Amos holds out hope for a REMNANT. Not all will be destroyed if repentance comes.
v.24             | KJV         | "mighty stream" (KJV) / "ever-flowing stream" (ESV) — Hebrew "nachal eitan" = a perennial stream (one that flows year-round, unlike seasonal wadis). Justice must be CONSTANT, not occasional.
v.26             | KJV         | "Moloch... Chiun" (KJV) / "Sikkuth... Kiyyun" (ESV) — Astral deities worshipped alongside the LORD. Israel mixed pagan star-worship with Yahweh worship.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Seek me and LIVE" (v.4). God offers life to the dying. Not rituals, not
   locations, not programs — seek HIM. The simplicity is the point. Where
   are you seeking life that isn't God?

2. "Let justice roll down like waters" (v.24). God measures your worship by
   your JUSTICE. How you treat the powerless IS your worship. Songs mean
   nothing if the poor are being crushed outside your church doors.

3. The Day of the LORD might not be what you expect (v.18). Be careful what
   you pray for. If you want God to "show up" but you haven't dealt with
   injustice and sin, His appearing will be darkness for YOU, not light.

4. God can HATE your worship (v.21). Let that sink in. Your church
   attendance, your giving, your praise songs — God can HATE them if they
   coexist with injustice. Religion without righteousness is revolting to
   God.

5. "Seek good, and not evil" (v.14). Moral simplicity: choose good. Hate
   evil. Establish justice. This isn't complicated theology — it's basic
   faithfulness applied to daily decisions.

6.notes:
Amos - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Woe to those at ease in Zion — the complacent rich who lounge on ivory beds, feast lavishly, and are not grieved over the ruin of the nation will be first to go into exile.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Woe to the Complacent
Key Themes: False security, luxury and indifference, "not grieved
for the affliction of Joseph," self-congratulation, God abhorring
pride, a nation raised up against Israel

Historical Context: Chapter 6 pronounces "Woe" upon the wealthy
elite of both Zion (Judah) and Samaria (Israel) who live in luxury
while ignoring the nation's spiritual collapse. They recline on
ivory beds, eat choice meat, invent music like David (but without
David's heart for God), drink wine by the bowlful — and are "not
grieved for the affliction of Joseph" (v.6). Their indifference
to suffering WHILE enjoying abundance is the specific sin condemned.
They boast of their own strength (v.13), but God will raise up a
nation to afflict them across their entire territory.

Structure:
- Woe to the Complacent: False Security (vv.1-3)
- The Luxurious Lifestyle (vv.4-6)
- First to Exile: The Self-Indulgent (v.7)
- God Abhors Jacob's Pride (vv.8-11)
- The Absurdity of Corrupted Justice (vv.12-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Revelation  | 3       | 17                 | Laodicea: "you say, I am rich... not realizing that you are wretched" (ESV)
v.1              | Luke        | 6       | 24                 | "Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation" (ESV)
v.4              | Luke        | 16      | 19                 | The rich man who "feasted sumptuously every day" while Lazarus starved at his gate
v.6              | Nehemiah    | 1       | 3-4                | Nehemiah: "When I heard... I sat down and wept." The opposite response to suffering
v.8              | Proverbs    | 16      | 18                 | "Pride goes before destruction" (ESV)
v.13             | Psalm       | 20      | 7                  | "Some trust in chariots... but we trust in the name of the LORD our God" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "beds of ivory" — Archaeological finds at Samaria confirm ivory-inlaid furniture in wealthy homes of this period.
v.5              | KJV         | "like David" — They compared their musical innovation to David's. But David composed for GOD's glory; they compose for their own entertainment.
v.6              | KJV         | "drink wine in bowls" — Not cups but BOWLS — implying excess. "Chief ointments" = the finest perfumed oils.
v.13             | KJV         | "a thing of nought" (KJV) — Referring to Lo-debar (meaning "nothing") — a town they conquered. They boast of capturing "Nothing" — unknowing irony.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Not grieved for the affliction of Joseph" (v.6). This is the sin of the
   comfortable. Are you aware of suffering around you — or has your comfort
   insulated you from caring? The test of spiritual health is not how much
   you HAVE but how much you GRIEVE for those who have not.

2. Complacency is not peace (v.1). Being "at ease" while injustice rages is
   not the peace of God — it is the numbness of self- absorption. True peace
   coexists with active compassion.

3. Your palaces built on pride will be delivered up (v.8). Whatever you
   build as a monument to your own greatness, God abhors. Wealth,
   achievement, reputation — built for self-glory, they become targets of
   divine judgment.

4. "First to go captive" (v.7). The self-indulgent go FIRST into exile.
   Those who were first in luxury will be first in judgment.

7.notes:
Amos - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God shows Amos three visions — locusts, fire, and a plumb line — and the priest Amaziah confronts Amos at Bethel, telling him to leave, to which Amos defends his divine calling as a simple herdsman sent by God.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Visions and Narrative — Locusts, Fire,
the Plumbline, and Amaziah's Opposition
Key Themes: God's mercy delaying judgment (twice), the plumbline
of absolute standard, confrontation with religious establishment,
the calling of a layman-prophet, the cost of speaking truth

Historical Context: Chapter 7 shifts from oracles to VISIONS and
NARRATIVE. Three visions show God's judgment: locusts (v.1), fire
(v.4), and a plumbline (v.7). Twice Amos intercedes successfully
and God relents (vv.1-6). But the third time — the plumbline — God
declares "I will not again pass by them" (v.8). The standard has
been measured; Israel fails. Then comes the dramatic confrontation
with Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, who tells Amos to go home.
Amos's response is one of the most powerful prophet-defenses in
Scripture: "I was no prophet... but the LORD took me."

Structure:
- Vision 1: Locusts — God Relents (vv.1-3)
- Vision 2: Fire — God Relents (vv.4-6)
- Vision 3: The Plumbline — No More Passing By (vv.7-9)
- Amaziah vs. Amos: The Confrontation (vv.10-13)
- Amos's Defense: "The LORD Took Me" (vv.14-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Genesis     | 18      | 23-32              | Abraham interceding for Sodom. Same pattern: prophet pleads, God responds to intercession
v.2              | Exodus      | 32      | 11-14              | Moses interceding for Israel after the golden calf
v.7              | 2 Kings     | 21      | 13                 | "I will stretch over Jerusalem the measuring line of Samaria, and the plumb line of the house of Ahab" (ESV)
v.8              | Isaiah      | 28      | 17                 | "I will make justice the line, and righteousness the plumb line" (ESV)
v.14             | 1 Corinthians | 1       | 27                 | "God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise" (ESV)
v.14             | 1 Samuel    | 16      | 11                 | David: taken from following the sheep. Same pattern

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "grasshoppers" (KJV) / "locusts" (ESV) — Same Hebrew word as Joel's plague. "King's mowings" = the first cutting of hay went to the royal treasury; the second growth was for the people. God sends locusts on the second growth — the people's portion would be entirely consumed.
v.7              | KJV         | "plumbline" — Hebrew "anak." A lead weight on a string used to test whether a wall is perfectly vertical. Symbolizes God's absolute moral standard against which everything is measured.
v.12             | KJV         | "eat bread" — Amaziah accuses Amos of prophesying for MONEY (eating bread = making a living). Amos refutes this: he didn't need the income; God called him.
v.14             | KJV         | "prophet's son" — Member of a prophetic guild/school (cf. 2 Kings 2:3-5, "sons of the prophets"). Amos was NOT professionally trained — purely divinely called.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Intercession can delay judgment (vv.2-3, 5-6). Twice God RELENTED because
   Amos prayed. Your prayers for others are not futile — they can change
   God's course of action. Pray for your family, church, and nation. It
   MATTERS.

2. The plumbline eventually comes (v.7). After mercy comes the STANDARD. God
   is patient, but He does measure. His plumbline is straight. How do you
   measure against His absolute standard? (Hint: only through Christ can
   anyone pass the plumbline test.)

3. "I was no prophet" (v.14). God doesn't call the qualified — He qualifies
   the called. Your lack of formal training, credentials, or pedigree is
   irrelevant when God SENDS you. Don't let Amaziahs disqualify what God has
   commissioned.

4. Religious leaders can be the greatest opponents of truth (v.12). Amaziah
   was a PRIEST — and he was silencing the prophet. Not all opposition comes
   from outside the church. Sometimes the establishment resists God's fresh
   word.

5. There is a "no more" with God (v.8). Don't presume on endless patience.
   There comes a moment when God says "no more passing by." Respond while
   the invitation is still open.

8.notes:
Amos - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The vision of a basket of summer fruit signals that Israel is ripe for judgment — God will turn their feasts to mourning, and a famine of hearing His word will come upon the land.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Summer Fruit and the Famine
of God's Word
Key Themes: The ripe fruit of judgment, exploitation of the poor,
corrupt merchants, the sun going down at noon, famine of hearing
God's Word

Historical Context: Chapter 8 presents the fourth vision: a basket
of summer fruit ("qayits") — a wordplay on "the end" ("qets").
Ripe fruit signals the END of the growing season; Israel is ripe
for judgment. The chapter then returns to the central theme of
Amos: economic exploitation of the poor. Merchants can barely wait
for the Sabbath to end so they can resume cheating (v.5). They
use dishonest scales, sell the sweepings of grain, and buy humans
for pocket change. The chapter climaxes with one of the most
devastating prophecies in Scripture: a famine not of bread but of
HEARING THE WORD OF THE LORD (vv.11-12). They will seek God's
word and NOT find it.

Structure:
- Vision 4: The Basket of Summer Fruit — The End (vv.1-3)
- Exploitation of the Poor: Dishonest Trade (vv.4-6)
- God Will Not Forget (vv.7-8)
- Cosmic Signs: Darkness at Noon (vv.9-10)
- The Famine of God's Word (vv.11-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | Nehemiah    | 13      | 15-18              | Sabbath-breaking for commerce
v.5              | Leviticus   | 19      | 35-36              | "You shall do no wrong in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity" (ESV)
v.9              | Matthew     | 27      | 45                 | "From the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land" (ESV). Darkness at noon — fulfilled at the cross
v.11             | 1 Samuel    | 3       | 1                  | "the word of the LORD was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision" (ESV)
v.11             | Psalm       | 74      | 9                  | "We do not see our signs; there is no longer any prophet" (ESV)
v.11             | Proverbs    | 29      | 18                 | "Where there is no prophetic vision the people cast off restraint" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "summer fruit" — Hebrew "qayits" (summer fruit) puns with "qets" (end). The basket of ripe fruit = the end has come. Wordplay is the prophetic message.
v.5              | KJV         | "the ephah small" = reducing the measurement given to buyers. "The shekel great" = increasing the price charged. "Falsifying balances" = rigged scales. Triple fraud: less product, higher price, dishonest measurement.
v.6              | KJV         | "sell the refuse of the wheat" — Hebrew "mappal" = the sweepings from the threshing floor — husks, dirt, debris mixed with leftover grain, sold to the poor as if it were quality.
v.11             | KJV         | "hearing" — Hebrew "lishmo'a" (to hear). Not just the absence of Scripture but the absence of HEARING — the ability to perceive God's voice. The famine is experiential.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "A famine of hearing the words of the LORD" (v.11). This is the worst
   punishment imaginable: God goes SILENT. You search everywhere — church,
   books, prayer — and hear NOTHING. Value God's word while you can hear it.
   Don't take Scripture, sermons, and conviction for granted. They can be
   withdrawn.

2. They couldn't wait for the Sabbath to end (v.5). Is your worship an
   interruption to your "real life"? Do you check the clock during the
   sermon, counting minutes until you can get back to your agenda? That
   reveals where your heart actually is.

3. God NEVER forgets (v.7). "I will never forget any of their works." Every
   dishonest transaction, every exploited person, every rigged scale is
   recorded. You may forget what you've done; God does not.

4. Darkness at noon (v.9). Even the brightest day can go dark when God
   withdraws. Don't trust in external circumstances — trust in God's
   presence. When He removes the light, no human energy can produce it.

9. “For, behold, I will command, and I will sift the house of Israel among all the nations, as grain is sifted in a sieve, yet not the least kernel will fall on the earth.
10. All the sinners of my people will die by the sword, who say, ‘Evil won’t overtake nor meet us.’
11. In that day I will raise up the tent of David who is fallen, and close up its breaches, and I will raise up its ruins, and I will build it as in the days of old;
12. that they may possess the remnant of Edom, and all the nations who are called by my name,” says Yahweh who does this.
13. “Behold, the days come,” says Yahweh, “that the plowman shall overtake the reaper, and the one treading grapes him who sows seed; and sweet wine will drip from the mountains, and flow from the hills.
14. I will bring my people Israel back from captivity, and they will rebuild the ruined cities, and inhabit them; and they will plant vineyards, and drink wine from them. They shall also make gardens, and eat their fruit.
15. I will plant them on their land, and they will no more be plucked up out of their land which I have given them,” says Yahweh your God.
9.notes:
Amos - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God declares there is no escape from His judgment — He will sift Israel among the nations yet not destroy them utterly, and the book closes with a promise to restore David's fallen booth and bring unprecedented agricultural blessing.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Amos, a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah who prophesied to
the northern kingdom (approximately 760-750 BC). Key theme: social
justice — God judges nations (including Israel) for oppressing the
poor. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a
mighty stream" (5:24).

Classification: Prophetic Vision and Oracle — Inescapable
Judgment and Glorious Restoration
Key Themes: No escape from God, the sifting of Israel, "not the
least grain shall fall," the tabernacle of David raised up,
Messianic restoration, permanent planting in the land

Historical Context: Chapter 9 is the FINALE of Amos — containing
both his darkest vision (vv.1-4: total inescapability of judgment)
and his brightest hope (vv.11-15: restoration of David's kingdom).
The vision opens with God STANDING on the altar — not in worship
but in JUDGMENT — commanding the destruction of the temple and
everyone in it. Nowhere is safe: not Sheol, not heaven, not the
sea floor, not captivity (vv.2-4). Yet the book does NOT end with
destruction. Verse 11 introduces the "tabernacle of David" — the
fallen Davidic dynasty that God will RAISE UP. James quotes v.11-12
at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:16-17) as fulfilled in Gentile
inclusion. The book ends with paradise: abundant harvest, rebuilt
cities, and permanent planting.

Structure:
- Vision 5: The LORD at the Altar — No Escape (vv.1-4)
- Doxology: The Sovereign God (vv.5-6)
- Israel Not Special in Judgment — But Preserved (vv.7-10)
- The Tabernacle of David Restored (vv.11-12)
- Paradise Restored: Abundance and Permanence (vv.13-15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Psalm       | 139     | 7-10               | "Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence?" (ESV)
v.2              | Jonah       | 1       | 3                  | Jonah tried to flee; God found him
v.9              | Luke        | 22      | 31                 | "Simon, Simon, behold, Satan demanded to have you, that he might sift you like wheat" (ESV). Sifting that preserves
v.11             | Acts        | 15      | 16-17              | James quotes this at the Jerusalem Council as justification for Gentile inclusion without circumcision
v.11             | Luke        | 1       | 32-33              | "the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David" (ESV)
v.13             | Leviticus   | 26      | 5                  | "Your threshing shall last to the time of the grape harvest, and the grape harvest shall last to the time for sowing" (ESV)
v.15             | Jeremiah    | 24      | 6                  | "I will plant them, and not pluck them up" (ESV). Same permanent-planting language
v.15             | Romans      | 11      | 29                 | "the gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable" (ESV). God does not revoke His promises

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Smite the lintel" — Strike the top of the doorpost so the entire structure collapses. The temple itself (probably Bethel) is being destroyed FROM God's own hand.
v.7              | KJV         | "children of the Ethiopians" — Hebrew "bnei Kushiyim." God challenges Israel's sense of superiority: in judgment, they are no more special than any other nation. Privilege does not prevent punishment.
v.9              | KJV         | "sieve" — Hebrew "kebarah" = a sieve that RETAINS the good (the stones/grain stay in) while the dirt/chaff passes through. The sifting PRESERVES value.
v.11             | KJV         | "tabernacle of David" — Hebrew "sukkath David" = booth/hut. Not a palace or temple but a BOOTH — indicating how far the dynasty has fallen. Yet God rebuilds even from this low point.
v.13             | KJV         | "mountains shall drop sweet wine" — Same imagery as Joel 3:18. Eschatological abundance characterizes the restored kingdom.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Not the least grain shall fall" (v.9). In God's sifting of your life —
   the shaking, the testing, the loss — nothing of TRUE value is lost. The
   chaff goes; the grain stays. Trust the Sifter. What remains after the
   shaking is what was real.

2. God rebuilds fallen things (v.11). Your "tabernacle" may be fallen — your
   ministry, your family, your dreams reduced to a hut. God RAISES ruins. He
   REPAIRS breaches. He REBUILDS. Bring Him your brokenness and watch Him
   work.

3. There is nowhere to hide from God (vv.2-4). You cannot outrun, outclimb,
   out-dive, or out-distance God. This is terrifying for the rebellious and
   comforting for the faithful. Either way: God is inescapable. Make peace
   with His presence now.

4. The book ends with PERMANENCE (v.15). "No more pulled up." After all the
   judgment, all the exile, all the shaking — God's FINAL word is permanent
   establishment. Your current instability is not your destiny. God's plan
   ends with you PLANTED, not pulled.

5. James applied v.11 to GENTILE inclusion (Acts 15). The "tabernacle of
   David" rebuilt means the door is open to ALL peoples. The restoration of
   God's kingdom is not exclusive — it draws in the nations.
